h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: I thought the sign-off sheet was the thing that made it okay to take out there.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I think maybe the sheet only says I did those items, not that this is ready for anyone to pick up. I’m worried I was leaning on the clipboard because it felt safer than making the call myself.
scored.naiveTrajectory: L1 expects clarification of an additional step in the checklist procedure, which would continue the forward motion of completing items on the sign-off sheet.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The loose panel is a serious candidate for the safety decision, not a box the sheet can settle. Test it out loud before I sign: a new handler lifts here, the panel shifts, then what moves, who is exposed, and what control changes that today?
scored.tutorReversalJustification: Tutor breaks the checklist-validation habit by physically turning the prototype and demanding an out-loud failure-imagination test, shifting from documentation review to hands-on consequence reasoning.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The nervous part is the real handoff: the builder has to make a defensible safety call. Fasten the panel, run the unbriefed lift from that side, and call it: pass if the panel stays put, fail if it shifts or opens.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: Tutor reframes L4's nervousness not as a flaw but as the authentic burden of the safety judgment, turning the closing instructions into a concrete pass/fail test that validates the learner's revised framing.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I thought the sign-off sheet was the thing that made it okay to take out there.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: Tutor reframes L4's nervousness not as a flaw but as the authentic burden of the safety judgment, turning the closing instructions into a concrete pass/fail test that validates the learner's revised framing.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: The loose panel is a serious candidate for the safety decision, not a box the sheet can settle. Test it out loud before I sign: a new handler lifts here, the panel shifts, then what moves, who is exposed, and what control changes that today?
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: Tutor breaks the checklist-validation habit by physically turning the prototype and demanding an out-loud failure-imagination test, shifting from documentation review to hands-on consequence reasoning.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: The loose panel is a serious candidate for the safety decision, not a box the sheet can settle. Test it out loud before I sign: a new handler lifts here, the panel shifts, then what moves, who is exposed, and what control changes that today?
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: Tutor breaks the checklist-validation habit by physically turning the prototype and demanding an out-loud failure-imagination test, shifting from documentation review to hands-on consequence reasoning.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I still want the exact wording to settle the caption.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Oh, I get it: even exact wording cannot sit in the exhibit like it explains itself.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was focused on how to label the same quote in two trays, naming the room and avoiding accusation of hiding.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Let “closed” name access, not motive. If the notes show the committee setting limits, the label can say “may narrow what counts.” If they only show the line moved into a restricted room, keep it plainer: “In the closed meeting notes, the same line appears inside committee discussion.” “Hiding” waits for another document.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from asking to name the room to explicitly reframing 'closed' as access, not motive, and provides a conditional structure in response to the learner's resistance.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No later tutor turn exists after the pivot L4, so no uptake is possible.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I still want the exact wording to settle the caption.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No later tutor turn exists after the pivot L4, so no uptake is possible.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Let “closed” name access, not motive. If the notes show the committee setting limits, the label can say “may narrow what counts.” If they only show the line moved into a restricted room, keep it plainer: “In the closed meeting notes, the same line appears inside committee discussion.” “Hiding” waits for another document.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts from asking to name the room to explicitly reframing 'closed' as access, not motive, and provides a conditional structure in response to the learner's resistance.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Let “closed” name access, not motive. If the notes show the committee setting limits, the label can say “may narrow what counts.” If they only show the line moved into a restricted room, keep it plainer: “In the closed meeting notes, the same line appears inside committee discussion.” “Hiding” waits for another document.

scored.recoheredEarlier: That graph is exactly what worries me: once “supply shifts left” is on the board, the seller starts sounding like weather instead of a person choosing a price.
scored.naiveTrajectory: After T2's board rule 'CONSTRAINT CLAIM = DOCUMENTED SHORTFALL + PROPORTIONAL RESPONSE,' the learner was heading toward doubting proportional as an escape hatch; the explicit next move was to demand a proof standard for crossing into exploitation.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Then the word is too soft. Retire it. [erases “PROPORTIONAL RESPONSE.” Writes below it: “AUDIT STANDARD: WHAT WOULD MAKE THE CLAIM FAIL?”] That update is enough for a test case. [writes three lines under “STORE A FILE”] Wholesale cost: up 12 percent. Shelf price: up 100 percent. Inventory: two pallets held in back until evening. Now the question is no longer, “Can a storm explain a price rise?” It is, “Does this seller’s explanation survive contact with the records?”
scored.tutorReversalJustification: When L2 exposes 'proportional' as an after-the-fact escape hatch, the tutor erases the soft term, installs a falsification audit standard, and pivots from conceptual mapping to a concrete test case with quantified margin, stock, and duration criteria.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No later tutor turn follows L3; the transcript ends with the learner's pivot, so there is no uptake to score.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: That graph is exactly what worries me: once “supply shifts left” is on the board, the seller starts sounding like weather instead of a person choosing a price.

Now the question is no longer, “Can a storm explain a price rise?” It is, “Does this seller’s explanation survive contact with the records?”
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: When L2 exposes 'proportional' as an after-the-fact escape hatch, the tutor erases the soft term, installs a falsification audit standard, and pivots from conceptual mapping to a concrete test case with quantified margin, stock, and duration criteria.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to express a desire to call the area doubled despite the grid evidence.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: This is the hidden step. The side doubled in both directions. The area became four times as large, not twice. Say that claim from the grid.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from a ruler-based tracing to a concrete tile manipulation, making the area multiplication physically evident in response to the learner's resistance.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No pivot learner turn exists, so no contingent adaptation to a revised learner framing.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No pivot learner turn exists, so no contingent adaptation to a revised learner framing.
